Article.ArticleTitle: Situating genetic counseling research: The role of the sociopolitical climate in research production and interpretation.

AffiliationInfo.Affiliation: Medical Sciences and Education, Boston University Chobanian and Avedisian School of Medicine, Boston, Massachusetts, USA.
Gertrude H. Sergievsky Center, Columbia University Irving Medical Center, New York, New York, USA.
Department of Genetic Counseling, MGH Institute of Health Professions, Boston, Massachusetts, USA.
Department of Medical Genetics and Psychiatry, Faculty of Medicine, University of British Columbia, Vancouver, British Columbia, Canada.

OtherAbstract.AbstractText: Genetic counseling research is affected by the policies and social attitudes around us. What we accept as “knowledge” depends on who has power and whose voices are heard. Sometimes, old ways of doing things remain, even after science or society changes. This can make it hard to update practices and help patients. Changes in laws and policies have a big impact on health care and research. Examples include new abortion restrictions in the United States or laws affecting LGBTQIA+ people. Patients might not feel safe sharing personal information or participating in studies because of discrimination or legal concerns. When important groups are left out, researchers miss patient needs and create gaps in knowledge. Funding priorities also shape what gets studied and whose health is given more attention. For example, disability research often looks for medical treatments instead of focusing on support and accessible services. To make research more useful, researchers need to think about how their beliefs and the world around them influence their work. Working closely with affected communities can help guide research decisions and prevent harm. Everyone, including researchers, healthcare providers, teachers, and journal editors, has a role to play in making sure research reflects and respects the needs of all individuals.
